message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To investigate COVID-19 epidemiology in Alberta, British Columbia and Ontario, Canada.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We calculated the incidence rate ratio (January 1—July 6, 2020) between the 3 provinces, and estimated time-varying reproduction number,
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    , starting from March 1, using EpiEstim package in R.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Using British Columbia as a reference, the incidence rate ratios in Alberta and Ontario are 3.1 and 4.3 among females, and 3.4 and 4.0 among males. In Ontario,
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    fluctuated ~1 in March, reached values >1 in early and mid-April, then dropped <1 in late April and early May.
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    rose to ~1 in mid-May and then remained <1 from late May through early July. In British Columbia,
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    dropped <1 in early April, but it increased towards the end of April.
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    <1 in May while it fluctuated around 1.0 in June and early July. In Alberta,
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    > 1 in March;
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    dropped in early April and rose again in late April. In much of May,
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    <1, but
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    increases in early June and fluctuates ~1 since mid-June.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    <jats:italic>
                      R
                      <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    </jats:italic>
                    wavering around 1.0 indicated that three provinces of Canada have managed to achieve limited onward transmission of SARS-CoV-2 as of early July 2020.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in Alberta, British Columbia, and Ontario, Canada, January 1-July 6, 2020

reference.unstructured: Marchand-Senecal X , Kozak R , Mubareka S , et al. Diagnosis and Management of First Case of COVID-19 in Canada: Lessons applied from SARS. Clin Infect Dis. 2020.
